article_title: Lenovo's Motorized Auto Twist Laptop Reaches US Retail in June at $1,649, Moving a Self-Rotating OLED Hinge From Concept to Product
summary: Accurate, fully-sourced Lenovo Auto Twist briefing; one spec misattribution (the 10-megapixel webcam cited to Engadget but actually in Notebookcheck) filed as a correction.

findings.message: The webcam spec 'The webcam is a 10-megapixel sensor' is attributed to Engadget, but the '10 MP webcam' figure does not appear in the Engadget snapshot — it appears in the Notebookcheck snapshot ('a 10 MP webcam with an IR sensor'), which is also a cited source.
findings.details: Recoverable: the 10MP webcam is accurate and present in a cited source (Notebookcheck), where the IR-sensor detail in the same sentence is also (correctly) attributed. Only the inline citation for the resolution points to the wrong outlet. Filed as a correction.

content_preview.title: Lenovo's Motorized Auto Twist Laptop Reaches US Retail in June at $1,649, Moving a Self-Rotating OLED Hinge From Concept to Product
content_preview.summary: The ThinkBook Plus Gen 7 Auto Twist, a 14-inch OLED laptop whose motorized hinge rotates the screen to follow the user, goes on sale in the US in June 2026 starting at $1,649.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Lenovo's ThinkBook Plus Gen 7 Auto Twist, a laptop whose motorized hinge turns the screen on its own, is moving from trade-show curiosity to a buyable product. According to [The Register](https://www.theregister.com/2026/01/07/lenovo_rollable_thinkpad/), "You'll be able to buy the ThinkBook Plus Gen 7 Auto Twist in June for a starting price of $1,649 in the US." [Notebookcheck](https://www.notebookcheck.net/From-concept-to-reality-Lenovo-ThinkBook-Plus-Gen-7-Auto-Twist-with-auto-rot...

editor_notes.content_quality: Clean, appropriately scoped Briefing (506 words) on the Lenovo ThinkBook Plus Gen 7 Auto Twist moving from concept to retail, with careful cross-outlet spec agreement.
editor_notes.source_verification: All 3 snapshots read from disk (HTTP 200). source-1 (The Register) confirms verbatim the '$1,649 in the US' starting-price quote and the 'The hinge can also use the webcam to guide its rotation so that it keeps you centered on video calls' quote, plus 500 nits, 1.4 kg / 3.09 lb, 2.8K, 120 Hz, the double-tap clamshell behavior, and the rollable-concept context. source-0 (Engadget) confirms the $1,649 price, the 'sometime in June' release, Core Ultra Series 3, 32GB/2TB/75Wh, the Thunderbolt 4 / USB-A 3.2 Gen 2 / HDMI / Wi-Fi 7 ports, and the knock-to-open behavior. source-2 (Notebookcheck) confirms the $1,649 / June 2026, the Core Ultra 300 (Panther Lake) identification, DDR5-9600, the 2.8K OLED / 120 Hz, 1.4 kg, and 'a 10 MP webcam with an IR sensor'.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Quotes are verbatim and nearly all specs are correctly attributed. The agent correctly resolved the $1,499-vs-$1,649 price conflict in favour of $1,649 (carried by all three cited outlets) and declined to assert a disputed hinge axis count. One subordinate misattribution: the 10-megapixel webcam is cited to Engadget but the figure is in Notebookcheck ('10 MP webcam'), not Engadget — filed as a correction. ('June 2026' attributed to Engadget is acceptable: Engadget says 'June' in a CES-2026 article and the year is corroborated by Notebookcheck.)
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional; 'What We Don't Know' honestly flags the un-asserted axis count, durability, and battery questions.
editor_notes.originality: New topic — no prior Lenovo Auto Twist / ThinkBook Plus Gen 7 coverage in the archive.
concerns: One subordinate spec misattribution (10MP webcam → Engadget vs Notebookcheck) — filed as a correction; the fact is accurate and in a cited source.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Accurate, well-sourced consumer-hardware briefing. APPROVE_WITH_CORRECTIONS for the single spec misattribution.
